questions.question: Which among the following explains India's "Quasi-Federal" nature of constitution?
options: Provision for more number of states
questions.explanation: Quasi federalism in constitution refers to 'federation with a centralising tendency'. Indian Constitution also contains many unitary or non-federal features- a strong Centre, single Constitution, single citizenship, flexibility of Constitution, integrated judiciary, appointment of state governor by the Centre, all-India services, emergency provisions.

questions.question: Which of the following British India acts made Governor-General of Bengal as Governor-General of India?
questions.explanation: Charter Act of 1833 made the Governor-General of Bengal as the Governor-General of India and vested in him all civil and military powers. Thus, the act created, for the first time, a Government of India having authority over the entire territorial area possessed by the British in India. Lord William Bentick was the first governor-general of India.

questions.question: First President of the constitutional assembly was elected on a temporary basis in line with practice of which country?
questions.explanation: The Constituent Assembly held its first meeting on December 9, 1946. Dr Sachchidan-and Sinha, the oldest member, was elected as the temporary President of the Assembly, following the French practice. Later, on December 11, 1946, Dr Rajendra Prasad and H C Mukherjee were elected as the President and Vice-President of the Assembly respectively.

questions.question: Totally there are how many scheduled languages?
questions.explanation: Originally, eighth schedule had 14 languages but presently there are 22 languages. They are: Assamese, Bengali, Bodo, Dogri (Dongri), Gujarati, Hindi, Kannada, Kashmiri, Konkani, Mathili (Maithili), Malayalam, Manipuri, Marathi, Nepali, Oriya, Punjabi, Sanskrit, Santhali, Sindhi, Tamil, Telugu and Urdu. Sindhi was added by the 21st Amendment Act of 1967; Konkani, Manipuri and Nepali were added by the 71st Amendment Act of 1992; and Bodo, Dongri, Maithili and Santhali were added by the 92nd Amendment Act of 2003.

questions.question: A person will be deprived of citizenship if he is ordinarily resident out of India continuously for how many years?
questions.explanation: The citizen has been ordinarily resident out of India for seven years continuously will be deprived of citizenship by the central government. But this will not apply if he is a student abroad, or is in the service of a government in India or an international organisation of which India is a member, or has registered annually at an Indian consulate his intention to retain his Indian citizenship.

questions.question: Right to property was scrapped of its fundamental rights status through which of the following amendment act?
questions.explanation: The right to property was deleted from the list of Fundamental Rights by the 44th Amendment Act, 1978. It is made a legal right under Article 300-A in Part XII of the Constitution. So at present, there are only six Fundamental Rights. They are 1.Right to equality (Articles 14 - 18) 2. Right to freedom (Articles 19 - 22) 3. Right against exploitation (Articles 23 - 24) 4. Right to freedom of religion (Articles 25 - 28) 5. Cultural and educational rights (Articles 29 - 30) 7. Right to constitutional remedies (Article 32).

questions.question: Originally, Part IVA of constitution had how many fundamental duties in its list?
questions.explanation: 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act (1976) included ten Fundamental Duties in Part IVA of the constitution. In 2002, one more Fundamental Duty was added. It was to provide opportunities for education to his child or ward between the age of six and fourteen years. This duty was added by the 86th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2002.

questions.question: Which among the following constitutional institution represents Indian federalism?
questions.explanation: The Constitution provides for a bicameral legislature consisting of an Upper House (Rajya Sabha) and a Lower House (Lok Sabha). The Rajya Sabha represents the states of Indian Federation, while the Lok Sabha represents the people of India as a whole. The Rajya Sabha (even though a less powerful chamber) is required to maintain the federal equilibrium by protecting the interests of the states against the undue interference of the Centre.

questions.question: President cannot exercise his veto power over which among the following bills?
questions.explanation: The President has the veto power over the bills passed by the Parliament10, that is, he can withhold his assent to the bills. But the President has no veto power in respect of a constitutional amendment bill. The 24th Constitutional Amendment Act of 1971 made it obligatory for the President to give his assent to a constitutional amendment bill.

questions.question: What is the fixed tenure provided under the constitution for the office of Director of CBI?
questions.explanation: The Director of CBI has been provided security of two-year tenure in office by the CVC Act, 2003. The Director of the CBI is appointed by the Central Government. The CBI is the main investigating agency of the Central Government. It plays an important role in the prevention of corruption and maintaining integrity in administration. It also provides assistance to the Central Vigilance Commission.

questions.question: Which of the following article is related to national emergency?
questions.explanation: Under Article 352, the President can declare a national emergency when the security of India or a part of it is threatened by war or external aggression or armed rebellion. A proclamation of national emergency may be applicable to the entire country or only a part of it. The President, however, can proclaim a national emergency only after receiving a written recommendation from the cabinet.

questions.question: The state list provided under seventh schedule is not applicable to which of the following state?
questions.explanation: As Jammu & Kashmir enjoys special status under the constitution, certain provisions applicable to other parts of India will not be applicable or will apply with some modifications. In that way, state list under seventh schedule is not applicable to J&K.

questions.question: To be recognized as Leader of opposition party in the parliament, what percentage of total strength of the house should a party win?
questions.explanation: In each House of Parliament, there is a 'Leader of the Opposition'. The leader of the largest Opposition party having not less than one-tenth or ten percentage of seats of the total strength of the House is recognised as the leader of the Opposition in that House. The main functions are to provide a constructive criticism of the policies of the government and to provide an alternative government. Therefore, the leader of Opposition in the Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha were accorded statutory recognition in 1977.

questions.question: A seat of a member of house of parliament can be declared vacant if the member remains absent for a period of how many days?
questions.explanation: A House can declare the seat of a member vacant if he is absent from all its meetings for a period of sixty days without its permission. In computing the period of sixty days, no account shall be taken of any period during which the House is prorogued or adjourned for more than four consecutive days.

questions.question: To be appointed as a Judge of Supreme Court, he/she should have held the office of Judge of a high court for a period of how many years?
questions.explanation: A person to be appointed as a judge of the Supreme Court should have been a judge of a High Court (or high courts in succession) for five years; or (b) He should have been an advocate of a High Court (or High Courts in succession) for ten years; or (c) He should be a distinguished jurist in the opinion of the president.

questions.question: Which among the following jurisdiction is especially enjoyed by Supreme court but not by High Court?
questions.explanation: The Constitution (Article 143) authorises the president to seek the opinion of the Supreme Court in the two categories of matters: (a) On any question of law or fact of public importance which has arisen or which is likely to arise. (b) On any dispute arising out of any pre-constitution treaty, agreement, covenant, engagement, sanad or other similar instruments. In the first case, the Supreme Court may tender or may refuse to tender its opinion to the president. But, in the second case, the Supreme Court 'must' tender its opinion to the president. In both the cases, the opinion expressed by the Supreme Court is only advisory and not a judicial pronouncement. This is referred to as advisory jurisdiction of supreme court.

questions.question: Who among the following appoints the advocate general of a state?
questions.explanation: The Constitution (Article 165) has provided for the office of the advocate general for the states.1 He is the highest law officer in the state. He/she is appointed by the governor.to be appointed, he/she must be a citizen of India and must have held a judicial office for ten years or been an advocate of a high court for ten years.

questions.question: Who among the following allocates various portfolios among the ministers?
questions.explanation: Prime Minister is the real executive authority in a parliamentary system. He/she is the head of the Union council of ministers. He allocates and reshuffles various portfolios among the ministers. He recommends persons who can be appointed as ministers by the president. The President can appoint only those persons as ministers who are recommended by the Prime Minister.

questions.question: Which among the following institution acts as the ombudsman system in Indian administrative setup?
questions.explanation: The Ombudsman is a constitutional authority and enjoys the powers to supervise the compliance of laws and regulations by the public officials. The Administrative Reforms Commission (ARC) of India (1966 - 1970) recommended the setting up of two special authorities designated as 'Lokpal' and 'lokayukta' for the redressal of citizens' grievances. The Lokpal would deal with complaints against ministers and secretaries at Central and state levels, and the lokayukta (one at the Centre and one in every state) would deal with complaints against other specified higher officials.

questions.question: Which among the following subject once in state list was transferred to concurrent list by 42ⁿᵈ Amendment Act of 1976?
questions.explanation: The 42nd Amendment Act of 1976 transferred five subjects to Concurrent List from State List, that is, (a) education, (b) forests, (c) weights and measures, (d) protection of wild animals and birds, and (e) administration of justice; constitution and organisation of all courts except the Supreme Court and the high courts. The rest of the above-mentioned subjects are still subjects of state list.

questions.question: Which among the following Union Territory is not a part of any of the zonal councils of India?
questions.explanation: The Zonal Councils are the statutory bodies created by an act of parliament. are advisory councils and are made up of the states of India that have been grouped into six zones to foster cooperation among them. Five Zonal Councils were set up. Andaman and Nicobar Islands, Lakshadweep are not members of any of the Zonal Councils. However, they are presently special invitees to the Southern Zonal Council.

questions.question: Who among the following doesn't take part in election to the office of President but have voting rights in removal of President?
options: Members of Legislative assemblies of UTs
questions.explanation: The President is the head of the Indian State. He/she is the first citizen of India and acts as the symbol of unity, integrity and solidarity of the nation. He/she is elected by members of electoral college consisting of: 1. the elected members of both the Houses of Parliament; 2. the elected members of the legislative assemblies of the states; and 3. the elected members of the legislative assemblies of the Union Territories of Delhi and Puducherry. The nominated members of either House of Parliament can participate in the impeachment of the President though they do not participate in his/her election.

questions.question: State having population less than what limit doesn't need to constitute an intermediate level in three tier panchayati raj system?
questions.explanation: The 73ʳᵈ Amendment act provides for a three-tier system of panchayati raj in every state, that is, panchayats at the village, intermediate, and district levels. Thus, the act brings about uniformity in the structure of panchayati raj throughout the country. However, a state having a population not exceeding 20 lakhs may not constitute panchayats at the intermediate level.

questions.question: In which year was the voting age brought down to 18 years from 21 years?
questions.explanation: The Indian Constitution adopts universal adult franchise as a basis of elections to the Lok Sabha and the state legislative assemblies. Every citizen who is not less than 18 years of age has a right to vote without any discrimination of caste, race, religion, sex, literacy, wealth, and so on. The voting age was reduced to 18 years from 21 years in 1989 by the 61st Constitutional Amendment Act of 1988.

questions.question: What is the total number of fundamental duties enshrined in Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: Article 51A deals with fundamental duties of the constitution. It consists of 11 duties. It originally consisted of 10 duties which were added through 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act in 1976. Providing opportunities for education to his child or ward between the age of six and fourteen years was added as a duty by the 86th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2002.

questions.question: An ordinary bill undergoes how many stages of reading in each house of parliament?
questions.explanation: An ordinary bill can originate in either House of the Parliament (Lok Sabha or Rajya Sabha). Such a bill can be introduced either by a minister or by any other member. The bill passes through three stages of reading in each house of parliament before submitted for assent of the President.

questions.question: Which among the following parliamentary terminology refers to termination of a sitting for an indefinite period?
questions.explanation: Adjournment sine die means terminating a sitting of Parliament for an indefinite period. When the House is adjourned without naming a day for reassembly, it is called adjournment sine die. The power of adjournment as well as adjournment sine die lies with the presiding officer of the House. He can also call a sitting of the House before the date or time to which it has been adjourned or at any time after the House has been adjourned sine die.

questions.question: What is the maximum age limit for the chairman of National Human Rights Commission can hold the office?
questions.explanation: The chairman and members of National Human Rights Commission hold office for a term of five years or until they attain the age of 70 years, whichever is earlier. After their tenure, the chairman and members are not eligible for further employment under the Central or a state government. The National Human Rights Commission is a statutory body, established in 1993.

questions.question: Disputes related to election to the office of Vice-President is dealt by which among the following authority?
questions.explanation: All doubts and disputes about election of the Vice-President are inquired into and decided by the Supreme Court whose decision is final. The election of a person as Vice-President cannot be challenged because the electoral college was incomplete (i.e., existence of any vacancy among the members of electoral college). If the election of a person as Vice-President is declared void by the Supreme Court, acts done by him before the date of such declaration of the Supreme Court are not invalidated.

questions.question: Part II of the constitution deals with which of the following provisions?
questions.explanation: The Constitution deals with the citizenship from Articles 5 to 11 under Part II. It identifies the persons who became citizens of India at the commencement of constitution. The other provisions regarding citizenship are provided by Citizenship Act, 1955, which has been amended in 1986, 1992, 2003 and 2005.
